specific_change: Downsize the RDS primary instance from db.r6g.2xlarge to db.r6g.large. Leave compute tier (m5.large × 6 with target-tracking ASG min=6/max=10) completely unchanged — compute is correctly sized and operating in healthy ranges. Update the Terraform aws_db_instance.database_primary instance_class from "db.r6g.2xlarge" to "db.r6g.large". This yields approximately $1,400/month in savings while preserving the 99.9% SLA.

conclusion.headline: Downsize database from db.r6g.2xlarge to db.r6g.large — save ~$1,400/mo

telemetry_observations: DB query P95 latency is 74.5ms (p95 of distribution), well under the 250ms SLA target.
DB connections P95 peaks at 40, indicating very low connection pressure for a 2xlarge instance.
DB I/O wait P95 is only 2.9ms, showing negligible storage bottleneck.
DB cache hit ratio stays above 97% (min 0.97), indicating working set fits comfortably in memory.
Compute CPU P95 is 77.8% at the 95th percentile of intervals — healthy utilization, not over-provisioned.
Compute memory P95 peaks at 71.7%, well within safe operating range.
Application P95 latency is 181ms at the 95th percentile, comfortably under the 250ms SLA.
infrastructure_context: Database is db.r6g.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 64 GiB RAM) costing $4,400/mo — 61% of total spend.
Compute is m5.large × 6 with ASG target-tracking (min=6, max=10) costing $2,800/mo.
Database has 500 GB allocated storage with encryption enabled.
No cache or network tiers are provisioned.

reasoning: The database tier consumes 61% of total monthly cost ($4,400 of $7,200) yet shows uniformly low utilization: query latency P95 at 74.5ms, connections peaking at 40, I/O wait under 3ms, and cache hit ratio above 97%. These metrics indicate the db.r6g.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 64 GiB) is significantly over-provisioned. A db.r6g.large (2 vCPU, 16 GiB) provides sufficient headroom given the low connection count and excellent cache hit ratio. Meanwhile, compute telemetry shows CPU P95 at ~78% and memory at ~70% — healthy utilization that confirms the m5.large × 6 configuration is correctly sized and must not be changed.

projected_state.notes: DB query latency may increase modestly (~10-20ms) on the smaller instance due to reduced CPU/memory, but remains well within the 250ms SLA budget. The 16 GiB RAM on db.r6g.large is sufficient given the 97%+ cache hit ratio and low connection count.

cost_impact.notes: Database tier drops from ~$4,400/mo (db.r6g.2xlarge) to ~$3,000/mo (db.r6g.large), saving ~$1,400/mo. Compute tier remains at $2,800/mo. Total: $7,200 → ~$5,800/mo. db.r6g.large on-demand pricing is roughly $0.4128/hr × 730 hrs ≈ $3,013/mo vs db.r6g.2xlarge at ~$1.6512/hr × 730 hrs ≈ $6,014/mo (list prices; actual $4,400 suggests partial RI/savings plan coverage, so savings may vary).

risk_assessment.primary_risk: Reducing from 64 GiB to 16 GiB RAM could degrade cache hit ratio if the working set grows, leading to increased I/O wait and query latency.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Monitor db_cache_hit_ratio, db_io_wait_p95, and db_query_p95_latency_ms closely for 2 weeks post-change. Set CloudWatch alarms: cache hit ratio < 95%, query P95 > 150ms, I/O wait > 10ms. Schedule the resize during a maintenance window (e.g., Sunday 04:00-06:00 UTC) to minimize user impact during the brief downtime.
risk_assessment.rollback: Modify the Terraform instance_class back to db.r6g.2xlarge and apply. RDS instance modification with immediate apply takes ~10-15 minutes. No data migration required.
risk_assessment.notes: Connection limit on db.r6g.large is lower than 2xlarge; verify max_connections parameter group setting accommodates the observed peak of 40 connections (db.r6g.large default is typically ~800, so this is not a concern).
